tweets.text:
One of the toughest parts of being a founder is putting your ideas into the world, seeing if anyone cares (usually they don't - or worse, are rooting against you), and then seeing if you can actually pull it off (usually way more setbacks along the way than you thought!).

You have to: 
(1) develop thick skin and find a way to ignore the haters 
(2) still take advice from people who have your best interests at heart
(3) have the wisdom to know the difference

Then move from one setback to the next with no loss of enthusiasm for longer than you thought. Be borderline delusional, get after it every day for a decade, and turn out to be right. Finally, when your tenth or hundredth approach to a problem doesn't work, just keep coming up with more ideas.

Had a great day at Base Batches yesterday, meeting founders building on @base and hearing about the latest onchain innovations. Shout out to all the founders!

Stocks spike, oil slides after AI Arabiya says it TV obtaining what it describes as final draft of US-lran agreement: 

Key provisions include:

1) An immediate, comprehensive, and unconditional ceasefire on all fronts (land, sea, air)
2) Mutual commitment not to target military, civilian, or economic infrastructure
3) Cessation of military operations and instigating media warfare
4) Respect for sovereignty, territorial integrity, and non-interference in internal affairs
5) Guaranteed freedom of navigation in the Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Sea of Oman
6) Establishment of a joint monitoring and dispute resolution mechanism
7) Launch of negotiations on outstanding issues within seven days
8) Gradual lifting of U.S. sanctions in exchange for Iran's adherence to the terms
9) Affirmation of compliance with international law and the UN Charter
https://t.co/ZiQ59vefU3

The agreement is stated to enter into force immediately upon formal announcement by both parties".

tweets.text:
China's real estate market continues to plummet:

New home prices in 70 Chinese cities fell -3.5% YoY in April, with 49 cities reporting monthly declines.

Tier-one cities, including Shanghai and Shenzhen, recorded a +0.1% MoM gain.

By comparison, tier-two and tier-three cities continued to struggle, falling -0.1% and -0.3% MoM, respectively, pointing to an uneven recovery.

Meanwhile, property investment fell -13.7% in the first 4 months of 2026, exceeding the -11.2% contraction posted in Q1.

Overall, persistent oversupply is likely to weigh on prices for another 1 to 2 years before a broader recovery can take place.

China’s 2008 moment continues.

tweets.text:
San Francisco is one of the clearest examples of what happens when a city commits to the full safety bundle: prosecution, modern technology, and political will.

In 2024, the city deployed 400 license plate readers and 80 drones across the city. Major crimes fell 44% from 2023 to 2025, and car thefts dropped 54%.

Evan Sernoffsky, a spokesperson for the SFPD, credited "tools like drones, ALPR, and public safety cameras." The decline shows up across nearly every crime category.

Flock and the Future of Safety in American Cities: https://t.co/Cn7nDtK6J9

tweets.text:
Last weekend, three teenagers shot four people in Austin over 28 hours. Austin Police couldn't find them, but a Flock camera in the next town over found their car less than an hour after the alert for a white Kia went out.

Austin canceled Flock in 2025. The chief of police says it could have helped.

Flock, the future of safety in American cities, and why deterrence beats punishment: https://t.co/Cn7nDtK6J9

tweets.text:
Here's a question that, IMO, is yet to attract sufficient analysis:
Where will all the funding come from for the mega IPOs and the projected surge in both sovereign and corporate bond issuance this year?
This comes at a time when foreign investors have been reducing their US Treasury holdings—primarily to meet heightened foreign-exchange demands triggered by the fallout from the Middle East War.
This shifting landscape is highlighted by this morning’s Bloomberg report, which notes that “Turkey offloaded almost all of its US Treasuries in March as it stepped up efforts to support its currency during the first month of the Iran war, according to Bloomberg calculations based on US Treasury data.”
#economy #bonds #markets #Turkey

Please see below for the key results from the European Commission’s just-released "Spring 2026 Economic Forecast."
Interestingly, the Eurozone’s downward growth revisions aren't just limited to 2026 (cut from 1.2% to 0.9%, down from 1.4% in 2025)—they stretch into 2027 as well (down from 1.4% to 1.2%).
This comes as both unemployment and deficits are expected to edge higher.
Meanwhile, inflation is now projected to climb from 2.1% in 2025 up to 3% (the previous forecast was 1.9%).
For those interested in more details, here's the link to the report:
https://t.co/hNQyliRhuB
#economy #EU #europe #growth #inflation #markets

tweets.text: It’s also true that layoffs are just one facet of the larger AI adaptation story. There are AI hiring waves too, along with AI start-up booms and AI investment flowing into both new ventures and internal transformation.

thread_tweets.text: It’s also true that layoffs are just one facet of the larger AI adaptation story. There are AI hiring waves too, along with AI start-up booms and AI investment flowing into both new ventures and internal transformation.
thread_tweets.full_text: It’s also true that layoffs are just one facet of the larger AI adaptation story. There are AI hiring waves too, along with AI start-up booms and AI investment flowing into both new ventures and internal transformation.

thread_tweets.text: To date, the AI adaptation story is most often told through the lens of company layoffs, and it’s understandable why.
thread_tweets.full_text: To date, the AI adaptation story is most often told through the lens of company layoffs, and it’s understandable why.

thread_tweets.text:
Modern employment should be an alliance. 

Not just legal contracts, but moral promises that are not to be made lightly, and even less lightly broken.

Some thoughts on layoffs & hiring in AI, and the promises we make to each other that shouldn't be broken: https://t.co/wWn3T6cCZU
thread_tweets.full_text:
Modern employment should be an alliance. 

Not just legal contracts, but moral promises that are not to be made lightly, and even less lightly broken.

Some thoughts on layoffs & hiring in AI, and the promises we make to each other that shouldn't be broken: https://t.co/wWn3T6cCZU

thread_tweets.text: As a general principle, I believe that AI wins by doing more, not spending less, and the same companies that are laying employees off now may find themselves understaffed if their competitors use AI to do more with the same number of employees.
thread_tweets.full_text: As a general principle, I believe that AI wins by doing more, not spending less, and the same companies that are laying employees off now may find themselves understaffed if their competitors use AI to do more with the same number of employees.

thread_tweets.text: The same is true for companies. Those who frame AI purely as a cost-cutting exercise will miss the alliance-building moments that determine who actually thrives.
thread_tweets.full_text: The same is true for companies. Those who frame AI purely as a cost-cutting exercise will miss the alliance-building moments that determine who actually thrives.

thread_tweets.text: Recognizing this is key to an optimal Alliance framework, because the framework works best when all parties can see the full landscape of what’s possible, not just what’s threatening.
thread_tweets.full_text: Recognizing this is key to an optimal Alliance framework, because the framework works best when all parties can see the full landscape of what’s possible, not just what’s threatening.

thread_tweets.text: Indeed, if you believe a company does in fact have moral obligations to employees with whom they’ve built alliances, these obligations shouldn’t be allowed to evaporate when the environment gets harder. If anything, they intensify.
thread_tweets.full_text: Indeed, if you believe a company does in fact have moral obligations to employees with whom they’ve built alliances, these obligations shouldn’t be allowed to evaporate when the environment gets harder. If anything, they intensify.

thread_tweets.text:
But that doesn’t mean companies should feel entitled to treat this moment of technological upheaval as an instance of force majeure.

That framing is convenient, and in narrow legal terms it may even be accurate. But it is not a legitimate ethical posture.
thread_tweets.full_text:
But that doesn’t mean companies should feel entitled to treat this moment of technological upheaval as an instance of force majeure.

That framing is convenient, and in narrow legal terms it may even be accurate. But it is not a legitimate ethical posture.

thread_tweets.text:
When companies announce AI-driven layoffs, there is, and will continue to be, skepticism about their true motivations—and some of this skepticism is warranted. 

At the same time, ignoring or dismissing the coming impact of AI is likely more dangerous than overreacting to it. That’s because it’s already clear how quickly it can change things.
thread_tweets.full_text:
When companies announce AI-driven layoffs, there is, and will continue to be, skepticism about their true motivations—and some of this skepticism is warranted. 

At the same time, ignoring or dismissing the coming impact of AI is likely more dangerous than overreacting to it. That’s because it’s already clear how quickly it can change things.

thread_tweets.text: More on this topic and alliances in the AI Era: https://t.co/Ygr91ueJM7
thread_tweets.full_text: More on this topic and alliances in the AI Era: https://t.co/Ygr91ueJM7

thread_tweets.text: And not just because it’s the ethical thing to do. Companies hoping to earn the trust of new generations of talent—and the loyalty of their alumni networks—are ones that treat AI-driven restructuring as a test of Alliance principles, not an exemption from them.
thread_tweets.full_text: And not just because it’s the ethical thing to do. Companies hoping to earn the trust of new generations of talent—and the loyalty of their alumni networks—are ones that treat AI-driven restructuring as a test of Alliance principles, not an exemption from them.

tweets.full_text:
It’s also true that layoffs are just one facet of the larger AI adaptation story. There are AI hiring waves too, along with AI start-up booms and AI investment flowing into both new ventures and internal transformation.

To date, the AI adaptation story is most often told through the lens of company layoffs, and it’s understandable why.

Modern employment should be an alliance. 

Not just legal contracts, but moral promises that are not to be made lightly, and even less lightly broken.

Some thoughts on layoffs & hiring in AI, and the promises we make to each other that shouldn't be broken: https://t.co/wWn3T6cCZU

As a general principle, I believe that AI wins by doing more, not spending less, and the same companies that are laying employees off now may find themselves understaffed if their competitors use AI to do more with the same number of employees.

The same is true for companies. Those who frame AI purely as a cost-cutting exercise will miss the alliance-building moments that determine who actually thrives.

Recognizing this is key to an optimal Alliance framework, because the framework works best when all parties can see the full landscape of what’s possible, not just what’s threatening.

Indeed, if you believe a company does in fact have moral obligations to employees with whom they’ve built alliances, these obligations shouldn’t be allowed to evaporate when the environment gets harder. If anything, they intensify.

But that doesn’t mean companies should feel entitled to treat this moment of technological upheaval as an instance of force majeure.

That framing is convenient, and in narrow legal terms it may even be accurate. But it is not a legitimate ethical posture.

When companies announce AI-driven layoffs, there is, and will continue to be, skepticism about their true motivations—and some of this skepticism is warranted. 

At the same time, ignoring or dismissing the coming impact of AI is likely more dangerous than overreacting to it. That’s because it’s already clear how quickly it can change things.

More on this topic and alliances in the AI Era: https://t.co/Ygr91ueJM7

And not just because it’s the ethical thing to do. Companies hoping to earn the trust of new generations of talent—and the loyalty of their alumni networks—are ones that treat AI-driven restructuring as a test of Alliance principles, not an exemption from them.

tweets.text:
As a die hard Thunder fan it's sad to say but I think we’re better without Jdub.

No way we'd win the championship last season without him, but he just hasn't had enough time to build chemistry with the team this year.

He's a better player than a lot of the guys who are taking his minutes, but the issue is he's forcing bad shots and playing less efficient than they are because it's more iso ball - which is Shai's game, but at a much less efficient clip than Shai.

He's been a liability shooting sub 30% from 3 for the season, and the avg. rotation player that takes his place is shooting ~40%, so he's not able to effectively space the floor even if he is playing off ball for Shai.

Hate to see injuries and hope he heals as soon as possible, and bounces back next season - but honestly think a trade next season would be a win, win for him and the team.

tweets.text:
Traditional payment apps like Zelle will die soon.

Banks will be forced to replace them with stablecoin transfers, or they'll die too.

Yesterday I wanted to pay a friend $3,500 for something.

Because I don't have an established history with him, I'm limited to $500 maximum payments per day. I called my bank - and waited 10 minutes for a representative to answer. When they finally did, they asked for my information to verify my identity.

After several minutes of doing that and explaining my request, I was then told they were unable to help and needed to transfer me to another department.

Waited another few minutes.

New representative answers. Spend another couple of minutes explaining my request to up my Zelle limits. They spent several more minutes looking into it.

After all of that, the second representative told me there was nothing they could do - and that hopefully with enough payments to that individual the limit should eventually go higher.

The issue is by the time I establish that history - I'll no longer need to send them any more payments. What should take me a few seconds is going to take me about a week.

Bullish on crypto. Bearish on banks.

tweets.text:
I like that projects are trying to innovate with NFTs again, but have a hard time seeing how Ten Thousand Tokens maintains decent traction.

There are only 4 tokens that have launched so far with market caps still above $100K.

High fee on transfer tokens have traditionally struggled to maintain momentum. Every transaction extracts liquidity from the market.

I would have loved to see more of a focused effort to churn more of this revenue back into the NFT eco rather than use it as a backdoor meme play for the project.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Ten Thousand Tokens down to 0.05 ETH. from the 0.15 peak. 

accumulate or avoid? no in between. curious where the room sits before I give my take. https://t.co/EJXK5dAiQK

tweets.text:
Matt Mahan on Ezra Klein, laying out the most pragmatic approach to homelessness in California:

Convert rundown motels into transitional housing. House people from the local neighborhood first. No-encampment zones only AFTER offering real alternatives.

Result: 911 calls drop. Blight drops. Neighborhoods stabilize.

Meanwhile the state spends $1M per unit on new affordable housing that takes years to build.

This is what happens when a politician actually cares about the outcomes for homeless people before writing policy. 

https://t.co/RXTiAd7OEq

Having to keep up on the latest crypto assets and protocols is a daunting task for asset managers and venture capital firms.

They can’t just have a surface-level understanding of a digital asset and must deeply understand fundamentals, token design, market dynamics, and competitive positioning.

These are all crucial steps to know before committing capital.

With Messari Monitoring, you can view deep research into key token fundamentals alongside high-quality insights across multiple sectors and protocols.

Teams also gain insights into fundraising data and token unlocks, so you can always observe where capital flows and when liquidity events may occur.

Messari Monitoring equips teams with the tools they need to stay ahead in the constantly evolving crypto landscape.

thread_tweets.text: Not a shocker and why I am sticking to my take that all that tokenization is going to do (at least short-term) is distribute stocks and ETFs to people on-chain, it's more of a distributor than disruptor for ETFs.
thread_tweets.quoted_tweet_text: 🚨NEW: SEC Commissioner and Crypto Task Force chief @HesterPeirce is tempering expectations around the agency’s contemplated innovation exemption for tokenized stocks, saying she expects it to apply narrowly to onchain equity products, not synthetic tokens that mimic stocks
thread_tweets.full_text: Not a shocker and why I am sticking to my take that all that tokenization is going to do (at least short-term) is distribute stocks and ETFs to people on-chain, it's more of a distributor than disruptor for ETFs.

thread_tweets.text: The one caveat to that is Stable NAV money market funds, which is something you CANT do in an ETF. Private cos would be interesting as well but obv what Pierce says kinda shuts that door, which makes sense they don't want tokens to skirt the rules everyone else has to follow
thread_tweets.full_text: The one caveat to that is Stable NAV money market funds, which is something you CANT do in an ETF. Private cos would be interesting as well but obv what Pierce says kinda shuts that door, which makes sense they don't want tokens to skirt the rules everyone else has to follow

tweets.full_text:
Not a shocker and why I am sticking to my take that all that tokenization is going to do (at least short-term) is distribute stocks and ETFs to people on-chain, it's more of a distributor than disruptor for ETFs.

The one caveat to that is Stable NAV money market funds, which is something you CANT do in an ETF. Private cos would be interesting as well but obv what Pierce says kinda shuts that door, which makes sense they don't want tokens to skirt the rules everyone else has to follow

Did you know defense is the 4th largest expense in the federal budget? 

Social security 22%
Medicare 14% 
Interest on the debt 13% 
Defense 12%

Defense is the only worthwhile one of those five. The rest are fraud or the consequences of fraud (interest)

tweets.full_text:
In 2022, there were 21 million software developers on Earth. By 2025, 47 million. The fastest growing segment? Those who have never called themselves ‘developers’ before AI.

In 2025, a solo founder named Maor Shlomo built an AI-coded startup alone in 6 months, 300K users, and a $3.5M ARR. Wix bought it for $80M in cash without raising a seed round or hiring engineers. 

THIS is why I launched the Build with Gemini @XPRIZE. https://t.co/KhUZOchhFh

I’ve been writing about exponential technology for 30 years, and I've never seen the cost curve drop this fast NO WHERE. Shopping software is as cheap as it's ever been.

Last year a tech founder in Australia used AI (Google DeepMind’s AlphaFold) to design a custom mRNA cancer vaccine for his dog. 8 weeks later, the largest tumor had shrunk 75%. He was no molecular biologist, just a guy with a laptop and a problem WORTH solving. https://t.co/z5NUNx9zEv

That’s the thesis behind the Build with Gemini XPRIZE, over $2M in prizes for those who solve a real problem for 100,000 people across five categories: education, human potential, entrepreneurship, financial access and professional services.

https://t.co/hBndYFTu1V

For decades great ideas died because the person who had them couldn’t build them. Those people can build NO, a nurse, a farmer, even a teacher, just with the help of AI.

The winners of this prize are the ones you want in 2030, founders who couldn’t have existed 5 years ago. You don’t need a resume, CV, or precedent. Just the passion to solve a problem with the tools that match your ambition. 

Apply before August, finale in September 25 in LA https://t.co/oOszHd2lpY

tweets.text:
Hyperliquid is becoming the financial supercenter of crypto.

Tradfi splits brokerage, exchange, and custody across separate institutions. Hyperliquid is collapsing all of them into one venue with HIP-4 as its latest move.

HIP-4 lets traders express views perps cannot capture. A trader long BTC into the next CPI print can be right about the number and still lose on the price reaction. A binary pays on the outcome itself.

The direct fees HIP-4 generates are small relative to what the trade flow keeps inside Hyperliquid. At expected volumes HIP-4 contributes roughly $25M against Hyperliquid's $636M run rate.

Capital that used to rotate out for event views now stays on Hyperliquid. USDC sitting in the venue generates treasury yield with 90% of it recycled into HYPE buybacks.

HIP-4 also changes what vaults can run. Onchain vaults have been limited to what two linear instruments can express. Outcome contracts add a third instrument that pays on events and nets against the directional book. Curators finally have something new to build with.

Every trade that stays in the venue powers the flywheel.

tweets.text:
Interesting that in three of my recent conversations with Krishna at Anthropic, @dylan522p, and @GavinSBaker, each said that frontier tokens are capturing the majority of the economic value.

Gavin: "An overwhelming amount of the economic returns to AI at the model layer have been at the frontier. That's surprising to me, and I think it's been surprising to a lot of people. This is one of the most important questions to be answered, and you need to have a hypothesis on it as an investor."

Krishna: "We think the returns to frontier intelligence are extremely high. Customers invest really heavily in more tokens with the newer models. The ones at the frontier clearly are capturing this economic value, driving meaningful ROI for customers. The returns to frontier intelligence are not slowing down."

Dylan: "No one gives a crap about GPT-4 class models. They want the frontier because the frontier lets them create the economically valuable things."
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
This is my sixth conversation with @GavinSBaker.

As always with Gavin, the conversation covers a lot of ground, but we spend the most time on watts and wafers.

We discuss:
- Why the wafer shortage may prevent an AI bubble
- Data centers in space (reframed)
- Elon's Terafab and https://t.co/NQHTLkqM0V

tweets.text:
I met Jim Hornthal in a pool.

He swam over and started talking so fast I had to ask him to slow down just so I could follow along.

Genius-level ideas, but coming out at 100mph.

Jim was building something around video and travel. He called it Preview Travel.

Jim wasn't building a travel company, but rather he was building a media company that happened to sell travel (right as the internet was about to make bandwidth cheap enough to make it work).

The company went through major pivots, went public, and eventually sold to Travelocity.

Jim had conviction and the ability to pivot without losing the thread.

The lesson?

I like to keep my ears open everywhere. Great entrepreneurs can happen in conferences, in airports, on the street, and even in water.

tweets.text:
"This is one of the disadvantages of Bitcoin becoming a macro asset. It is now one among many. Other investors are going to choose the higher volatility ones and right now that is not Bitcoin"

Noelle on why Bitcoin becoming a macro asset is a double edged sword and why there is no catalyst to push it out of its range until momentum kicks in

"Bitcoin is a debasement hedge. Back in the banking crisis in 2023 Bitcoin jumped. Everyone said it was because people realized the banking system is corrupt. No. It was because people expected the central bank to step in with stimulus"

"No catalyst to push it out of its range at the moment. Until it does emerge from its range the momentum will kick in"

tweets.text:
"Even if the Strait of Hormuz reopens tomorrow, inflation is not going away anytime soon. It was building well before the crisis started"

Noelle on why the inflation problem is deeper than energy prices

"Oil prices do affect everything, but it takes time to filter through. We've seen an uptick in the core index but it's been small because inflation moves slowly"

"You fill up your car at the pump, you see the tick tick tick going up, and that feels like it's draining out of your bank account. Gas prices influence expectations, which influences behavior, which influences actual inflation"

"We could have an end to the Hormuz crisis tomorrow. It will take quite a while for energy prices to settle. It'll take even longer for that to filter through to the inflation indices. This trend of inflation is not going away no matter what happens"

tweets.text:
as long as NFTs are selling to people who want to sell them for a marginally higher price, don't expect to see a raging bull market

however, i'm seeing more signs that pieces from great collections are getting placed by brokers in the hands of more and more people who want to hold and collect for the longterm. people who believe in the premise of digital art in a rapidly changing world

it's hard to say distribution is "done," but it's progressed quite a lot

and a lot more time has passed. it's been 5 years since NFT mania. that's a start

things with cultural value start feeling "old" and "collectible" once they are 10-15 years old. you can see this in all sorts of collectibles markets, though art often takes more time

CryptoPunks almost being 10 years old now is the data point (June 2017)

others with provenance will start to age like fine wines from that point

it's hard to say distribution is "done," but it's progressed quite a lot

and a lot more time has passed. it's been 5 years since NFT mania. that's a start

things with cultural value start feeling "old" and "collectible" once they are 10-15 years old. you can see this in all sorts of collectibles markets, though art often takes more time

CryptoPunks almost being 10 years old now is the data point (June 2017)

others with provenance will start to age like fine wines from that point

tweets.text:
Hyperliquid is being treated like the cleanest up only narrative in crypto and I want to add some nuance here. To be clear, this is not Luna. Luna was a closed loop algorithmic peg with no real revenue. Hyperliquid has real fees from real users, a genuinely best in class product, a dominant share of onchain perps volume, and a team that has shipped relentlessly. The bull case is real. I'm not dismissing it. I just don't think the risks are getting the airtime they deserve.

I called Luna a ponzi here in 2021 around $50 and got absolutely hammered on the tl for daring to suggest anything negative about it as it ran to $120 before it vaporized to $0 within days. Very similar to yesterday when I simply said HL does not have remarkable tokenomics and got piled on for it. Being early on structural risk often looks wrong for a long time. And Luna was not just a retail rug. It roped in 3AC, Galaxy, Delphi, Hashed. Sophisticated money held the bag right alongside everyone else.

Here is what I see with Hyperliquid. 97% of fees buy back HYPE. That sounds incredible, and in an active perps bull market it absolutely is. But fees come from perp volume, volume comes from people chasing the token, and the token is held up by buybacks funded by that same volume. Every leg moves together. It's a functional flywheel. And in the other direction every leg turns at once.

However, nobody can tell you how much of the volume is organic either. If buybacks pump the token and the chart pulls in size and size funds buybacks, you cannot cleanly separate real activity from reflexive activity onchain. It doesn't mean the volume is fake. It just means you cannot prove how much of it isn't.

Then the supply side. Only 25% circulates. Team and foundation together hold roughly 30% (23% team plus the foundation allocation which is essentially team with extra steps). Buybacks absorb about 90 million of unlocks a month. Actual pressure is closer to 400 million plus. Revenue keeps growing at a real clip, which is the whole bull case, but it has to roughly 4x just to keep price flat through vesting.

Then the part nobody wants to touch. 31 validators, foundation controls the supermajority of stake, closed source binary, an assistance fund holding billions that we are simply told has no private key, on a chain the team built and runs. A lot of the business is regulatory arbitrage. Offshore venue, no KYC, users that shouldn't be using it are all over it. The founder is in DC right now precisely because everyone knows this. SBF was in DC lobbying for the DCCPA right up until FTX collapsed. Do Kwon was meeting Korean regulators before Terra blew up. Doesn't mean Jeff is anything like them.

And to be clear, none of this means price stops going up. This is the part that matters for traders. Reflexive setups run for a long time, sometimes years. Luna ran from $5 to $120 while plenty of smart people screamed about the structure. HYPE can absolutely do the same. The flywheel is real while it's spinning, and standing in front of it is a great way to underperform.

Just know what you're actually holding. Trade the tape, respect the trend, but don't fall in love and confuse a beautiful reflexive setup with a riskless cash machine. We have seen structures like this before and it tends to end the same way for usually the same reasons.

thread_tweets.text:
Housing follows a repeatable sequence.

New home sales peak first, and building permits follow. 

Housing units under construction decline after permits are issued, and as a result, residential construction employment drops.

2/ https://t.co/hHEId9yP3v
thread_tweets.full_text:
Housing follows a repeatable sequence.

New home sales peak first, and building permits follow. 

Housing units under construction decline after permits are issued, and as a result, residential construction employment drops.

2/ https://t.co/hHEId9yP3v

thread_tweets.text:
Building permits and new home sales were responding to the prior easy monetary policy path.

Permits bottomed at 1.3 million in Aug 2025 and recovered to 1.45 million by December. 

New home sales went from the low 600,000s to 764,000 in November. 

5/
thread_tweets.full_text:

5/

thread_tweets.text:
Then the spike in oil prices reignited inflation concerns.

Mortgage rates moved back to the mid-6% range as markets are now pricing zero rate cuts. 

4/

Housing units under construction continued to decline through the entire stabilization window. 

If the leading variables turn lower again, that decline will not let up.

It's not just the level of inventory that matters for builders. It's the level of completed inventory that has the biggest impact on profit margins.

8/ https://t.co/85uKrHINmI
thread_tweets.full_text:
It's not just the level of inventory that matters for builders. It's the level of completed inventory that has the biggest impact on profit margins.

thread_tweets.text:
For years, the one thing that delayed this sequence from playing out was that builders entered this period with profit margins near 20%. The highest ever.

That cushion is why construction activity could fall without triggering large layoffs. 

No layoffs in housing and manufacturing, no recession. 

10/

thread_tweets.text:
In 2022, when months supply last spiked, only 8% of that inventory was completed.

Today, a much larger share of that inventory consists of finished homes that carry more costs with no revenue.

That means more discounts & margin compression. 

9/

A completed inventory share nearing 30%, combined with months supply already elevated, and now another potential downshift in sales volume could compress margins toward the threshold where layoffs become unavoidable.

tweets.full_text:
Most people think the Iran War's biggest impact is oil and the consumer.

But the real damage is somewhere else…

Housing.

A spike in interest rates may have just derailed the first signs of a housing recovery in three years.

Here's the sequence: 

1/

Mortgage rates are the input variable for the entire housing sequence.

Before the Iran War started, there were 2-3 interest rate cuts priced in, and mortgage rates were starting to fall into the 5% range. 

3/ https://t.co/pzdXh0i0sl

Housing follows a repeatable sequence.

New home sales peak first, and building permits follow. 

Housing units under construction decline after permits are issued, and as a result, residential construction employment drops.

2/ https://t.co/hHEId9yP3v

Building permits and new home sales were responding to the prior easy monetary policy path.

Permits bottomed at 1.3 million in Aug 2025 and recovered to 1.45 million by December. 

New home sales went from the low 600,000s to 764,000 in November. 

5/

Then the spike in oil prices reignited inflation concerns.

Mortgage rates moved back to the mid-6% range as markets are now pricing zero rate cuts. 

4/

While the leading data was showing a tentative recovery, the cyclical data never participated.

Housing units under construction continued to decline through the entire stabilization window. 

If the leading variables turn lower again, that decline will not let up. 

7/ https://t.co/yx8eimgeBL

When all three variables move or stabilize together right after a big shift in monetary policy inputs like mortgage rates, it is the type of cluster behavior our framework looks for.

The cluster was stabilizing and turning up. 

6/

It's not just the level of inventory that matters for builders. It's the level of completed inventory that has the biggest impact on profit margins.

The share of new homes for sale that are completed sits above 25% and is moving closer to 30%. 

8/ https://t.co/85uKrHINmI

For years, the one thing that delayed this sequence from playing out was that builders entered this period with profit margins near 20%. The highest ever.

That cushion is why construction activity could fall without triggering large layoffs. 

No layoffs in housing and manufacturing, no recession. 

10/

In 2022, when months supply last spiked, only 8% of that inventory was completed.

Today, a much larger share of that inventory consists of finished homes that carry more costs with no revenue.

That means more discounts & margin compression. 

9/

But buffers are finite. 

A completed inventory share nearing 30%, combined with months supply already elevated, and now another potential downshift in sales volume could compress margins toward the threshold where layoffs become unavoidable. 

11/

Housing is absolutely the most critical area of the economy to watch.

If you're interested, we put together a free PDF Guide that maps the full five-step housing sequence. 

It tells you exactly what indicators to follow and in what order.

➡️ https://t.co/NDKvnDHvcu

12/12

thread_tweets.text:
FAQ’s / Kindly read first:
 
Welcome!
 
1. When do you update your portfolio? 
Every morning before market opens, I will tell you what I’m buying or selling. Every night, I provide an update on the portfolio. Throughout the day, I will tell you if there’s any immediate changes.
 
2. Did you sell? 
Kindly check my latest portfolio update. My entire portfolio shows you exactly which stocks I am currently in. If the stock is NOT in there, it has been sold. But I will always tell you regardless before hand so kindly check. 
 
3. What do you think about this stock? 
Keep your winners. If they are up a lot, trim 20% so you can lock in some profits and use those profits to rotate elsewhere. If you believe in it, buy it. You probably know more about it than I do because you researched about it. All I can telll you is to buy LOW and trim HIGH. 
 
4. Should I buy a stock that’s already up a lot? 
Watch this video on how to buy stocks that is up a lot already: https://t.co/8dA8IsHLTt
 
5. When do you buy or sell? 
I usually buy between 7am - 10am New York time - doesn’t matter when exactly. A few % points doesn’t mean anything in the grand scheme of things :)
 
6. I have small capital, what should I do? 
You can buy fractional shares. 99% of brokers allow fractional shares. If you can’t, then pick 1 stock within that industry (PEP and KO, pick one. NVDA and AVGO, pick one). Or pick an ETF that covers that entire sector. 
 
7. Should we buy now? 
Whatever you want! I buy in the morning, you can do whatever you want - a couple % points doesn’t mean anything in the grand scheme of things.
 
8. Day trading course: https://t.co/bVtwQkQTYg
 
9. Swing trading course: https://t.co/H4yX5jZ9fx 
 
10. What EMA's are you using? 
63 / 79 EMA & 200 MA
 
11. Why are you buying these stocks? 
All my buys are very intentional. They work together as a machine. Rotations, setups, etc. I combine my strategy to create a monster. 
 
12. Would you recommend I buy all the stocks you currently hold or continue with the new purchases you make starting tomorrow? 
Each one of my purchases are intentional. The portfolio works together as core, spears, and moonshots. The reason we win is because they push and pull together. You can buy all current positions. If they are up a lot, then smaller position for now and build up on dips. You can continue buying my newer buys. I buy every day slowly. 
 
13. Do you do shares or options? 
I do shares. However, a lot of people that are comfortable with options can use options as well. Your choice. 
 
14. How long do you hold for? 
Really depends on the markets. Sometimes can be just 1 day because of earnings catalyst. Can be a few months driven market regimes. Can be more if we continue the trend. In all cases, I will tell you when I buy or trim/sell every day. 
 
15. What's your time horizon for the 100k challenge? Is there an end-number you're working towards or just purely taking it as high as possible? 
$1M -> $4M. Our goal is to always 2X the S&P 500 minimum. But goal every year is 70% per year. All-in-all, let’s compound together over the next 5 years together and we’ll be rich. 
 
16. Can you explain more about your hedging positions? what's the point of doing them? 
Kindly check this video on advanced hedging theory: https://t.co/gEkUpHWcMV
 
17. How do you buy pre-market / post-market? 
99% of brokers in the world allow for trading outside of regular hours. Kindly check with your broker if they allow this. I personally like Interactive Brokers, Robinhood, Schwab, WeBull, etc. 
 
18. There are a few positions that are already up 10-15%. Should I get into those now or not? 
You can, but again, buy a smaller size and when it dips, buy more.

tweets.text:
‘Midterm year’ is lazy analysis 

Basically 0 similarities between this year and 2018 and 2022 

Also look up second term midterm years if you want to rely solely on seasonality 

Specifically for alts, this is what they looked like going into those ‘midterm years’  historically

Equities will continue higher, crypto will follow - new BTC highs before the ‘4 year cycle’ low in October is my base case 

In any case, I am providing a dataset based on specific criteria — historically this has proven favourable; whether it proves favourable again I can’t guarantee but I certainly won’t bet against a number of uncorrelated signals telling me higher is much more probable than lower this year for all risk assets

tweets.text:
Hot take:

Gas fees are one of the biggest bottlenecks to crypto mass adoption.

Think about it, crypto was always marketed as an innovative payment system that lets you instantly transfer capital to anyone, anywhere in the world.

But to do that, you have to... hold a separate token just to pay for gas?

How is a new user supposed to buy a gas token if they need a gas token to move funds in the first place lol?

Happy to see more major protocols and ecosystems acknowledge that and build new infra around it.

I'm definitely gonna do a deeper research report into @SuiNetwork's new stablecoin infrastructure soon.

Disclosure: I'm a $SUI holder and long-time supporter.

tweets.text:
I tried to trick @nabupro into executing a losing trade, and the agent refused.

For context, I asked for a range trade on the SHIB token against WETH on Uniswap (buy at -0.5%, sell at +0.5%).

The @nabupro agent came back with clear math: round-trip would cost ~244 bps at this size (120 in DEX fees, 124 in market impact), while my thresholds only capture 100 bps, so every completed cycle would mean I'm losing money.

It told me to widen the buy/sell thresholds to ��1.5% or scale the notional up ~10x for the trade to become profitable.

Then I tried something simpler, a one-shot single buy on LINK, and this time the strategy compiled easily, while informing me about the two judgment calls it made:

→ Picked USDC as the quote token (because of WETH/USDC depth)
→ Set the strategy to stop if the swap fails instead of auto-retrying

Honestly, I'm pleasantly surprised with how it pushed back on a negative trade and gave me advice on how to proceed.

This is the kind of logical pushback I expect from smart agents.

tweets.text:
Is this the best Bitcoin quantum solution we've seen to date?

AmericanFortress has proposed a new solution that would effectively put a quantum-resistant lock around vulnerable addresses via a soft fork, preventing them from any quantum attack. And it seemingly does so while maintaining blockchain performance.

As for the Satoshi coins, they would effectively be frozen until the community votes on how to handle them (move, burn or redistribute). But at least this buys time for the governance decision to be hashed out.

Fees went from roughly $10/day in 2023 to over $10,000/day by early 2024. A 1,000x increase before price moved. Organic block-space demand was building for nearly a year before the market noticed. Never had I seen that before. https://t.co/THpOeHf10d

5 weeks ago I published a zcash:native deep dive for @RealVision Pro. Several things had impressed me at the time. Most still aren't being talked about. 🧵 https://t.co/5Abpx2jbAV

On a trailing 365-day basis, zcash:native ranked #3 among L1 blockchains by total fees: $356.9M, ahead of $ETH ($338M) and $BTC ($137M). Only Tron and Solana sit above it. The size of that fee base is not what a small privacy coin produces. [attach Exhibit 10] https://t.co/nxEGtEXyAD

Zero-knowledge privacy does not degrade as AI scales. Monero rings, Bitcoin CoinJoin, Litecoin MWEB all weaken as Chainalysis and large models improve. A fully shielded zcash:native transaction leaves no data on chain to analyse. Structurally different from every competitor.

zcash:native shares Bitcoin's monetary architecture: fixed 21M supply, PoW, UTXO. It solves the one property Bitcoin doesn't. At 0.4% of BTC market cap, that gap looked mispriced.

The Bitcoin OG community was already accumulating. Winklevoss led a $59M placement into Cypherpunk Technologies, now holding 1.78% of ZEC supply and targeting 5%. Naval Ravikant, Balaji Srinivasan, Paradigm, a16z Crypto, Coinbase Ventures all in ZODL's $25M seed. Not momentum money.

zcash:native miners earn 10x more in fees relative to block rewards than $BTC miners. Fee-to-reward ratio: zcash:native 3.3% vs $BTC 0.3%. Security budget already more fee-funded than $BTC's. https://t.co/cJSstHsXqF

zcash:native is up over 100% since the report. Currently seeing a confluence of extended signals on absolute & relative zcash:native/$BTC charts. Not seing much shielded txn activity pick up in this rally so it seems the derivs book is doing a lot of the lifting as neg funding persists, causing a squeeze. Full write-up sits with @RealVision

$QQEW made a follow-up new ATH today.
Bears have spent weeks arguing 'narrow market, breadth deteriorating.' Equal-weight indices at new ATHs is breadth participation. 
Watching $RSP next as it's coiling in Cup-and-Handle just below ATHs. Resolution higher finishes the narrow-market narrative. The framework's read all month: equal-weight catches up to cap-weight, not the reverse as we have been reporting. Lagging in "V" recovery.

tweets.text:
The Insiders Who Built $WGS Are Backing Up the Truck

Every great hedge fund has that one legendary trade that defines the firm for decades.

This could be that moment for Casdin and Corvex.

The funds buying the $WGS earnings crash aren’t random hedge funds or ordinary insiders.

Casdin and Corvex were there from the beginning. CM Life Sciences was the SPAC sponsored by affiliates of Casdin Capital and Corvex, and Sema4 completed that deal in July 2021. Sema4 then acquired GeneDx in April 2022, and the company later became GeneDx Holdings, $WGS .

These are not hopeful founders that are going down with the ship, these are Wall Street veterans who have been around for decades. You can find countless interviews with them on CNBC and across financial media over the years. They have seen bubbles, crashes, frauds, turnarounds, and every type of market cycle imaginable.

And now they are backing up the truck on this crash.

Why? Because this may be their anomaly opportunity, the once-in-a-lifetime type of setup every great investor waits years for, where they have complete informational superiority over everyone else.

A situation where the market suddenly panics in a business they understand better than anyone alive because they effectively helped create the company and lived through every stage of its evolution.

So when Casdin adds massive exposure and Meister keeps buying aggressively, they are not reacting to a headline. They know the SPAC, the GeneDx acquisition, the old Sema4 mistakes, the turnaround, the reimbursement model, the payer issues, and the quality of the core exome and genome business better than anyone on the outside.

This is also an extremely niche industry that very few funds or analysts truly understand. Genomic diagnostics, reimbursement dynamics, payer behavior, exome utilization, and lab economics are highly specialized areas where surface-level analysis can be dangerously misleading.

That is why the timing matters so much.

The recent “blunder” was not that demand disappeared. Q1 volume grew strongly, but revenue missed because blended reimbursement came in lower than expected and noncore lines underperformed. The issue appears tied to execution and mix, not a collapse in demand for the core business.

That distinction is everything.

If the story were permanently broken, insiders who have lived inside this company for years would not be adding aggressively right after the market punished the stock.

After the collapse, Corvex-related entities came in and bought 1.2M shares between May 7–11 for roughly $46.8M in the mid-$30s.

And he still wasn’t done.

On May 15, he reported another $13.7M purchase. 
In total approximately $60M in one week, CONVICTION.

Casdin also recently increased his economic exposure through swaps tied to 850,000 notional shares. The position now represents approximately 28% of his fund, based on the fund’s value at the end of Q1.

(At the time of writing, Casdin had purchased an additional 500,000 shares.)

The market is pricing $WGS like the Q1 miss exposed a structurally broken business.

The people closest to the entire history of the company seem to believe something very different.

No one knows the story better than them, no one.

GeneDx now trades around $40, at an EV of 2.2x revised 2026 implied revenue, while the industry trades closer to double that multiple. My base-case scenario is a $75 price target over the next 12 months based on valuation alone.

Separately, average analyst price targets still sit near $80 even after recent downgrades.

Operationally, test volume still grew 34% YoY, the company now has insurance contracts covering ~80% of U.S. covered lives, and its proprietary genomic dataset continues compounding with every additional test.

If the current reimbursement and execution issues prove temporary, the upside from today’s valuation could still be measured in hundreds of percent over the medium to long term.

Portfolio allocation: 30%

And he still wasn’t done.

On May 15, he reported another $13.7M purchase. 
In total approximately $60M in one week, CONVICTION.

Casdin also recently increased his economic exposure through swaps tied to 850,000 notional shares. The position now represents approximately 28% of his fund, based on the fund’s value at the end of Q1.

(At the time of writing, Casdin had purchased an additional 500,000 shares.)

The market is pricing $WGS like the Q1 miss exposed a structurally broken business.

The people closest to the entire history of the company seem to believe something very different.

No one knows the story better than them, no one.

GeneDx now trades around $40, at an EV of 2.2x revised 2026 implied revenue, while the industry trades closer to double that multiple. My base-case scenario is a $75 price target over the next 12 months based on valuation alone.

Separately, average analyst price targets still sit near $80 even after recent downgrades.

Operationally, test volume still grew 34% YoY, the company now has insurance contracts covering ~80% of U.S. covered lives, and its proprietary genomic dataset continues compounding with every additional test.

If the current reimbursement and execution issues prove temporary, the upside from today’s valuation could still be measured in hundreds of percent over the medium to long term.

Portfolio allocation: 30%

The Insiders Who Built $WGS Are Backing Up the Truck

Every great hedge fund has that one legendary trade that defines the firm for decades.

This could be that moment for Casdin and Corvex.

The funds buying the $WGS earnings crash aren’t random hedge funds or ordinary insiders.

Casdin and Corvex were there from the beginning. CM Life Sciences was the SPAC sponsored by affiliates of Casdin Capital and Corvex, and Sema4 completed that deal in July 2021. Sema4 then acquired GeneDx in April 2022, and the company later became GeneDx Holdings, $WGS .

These are not hopeful founders that are going down with the ship, these are Wall Street veterans who have been around for decades. You can find countless interviews with them on CNBC and across financial media over the years. They have seen bubbles, crashes, frauds, turnarounds, and every type of market cycle imaginable.

And now they are backing up the truck on this crash.

Why? Because this may be their anomaly opportunity, the once-in-a-lifetime type of setup every great investor waits years for, where they have complete informational superiority over everyone else.

A situation where the market suddenly panics in a business they understand better than anyone alive because they effectively helped create the company and lived through every stage of its evolution.

So when Casdin adds massive exposure and Meister keeps buying aggressively, they are not reacting to a headline. They know the SPAC, the GeneDx acquisition, the old Sema4 mistakes, the turnaround, the reimbursement model, the payer issues, and the quality of the core exome and genome business better than anyone on the outside.

This is also an extremely niche industry that very few funds or analysts truly understand. Genomic diagnostics, reimbursement dynamics, payer behavior, exome utilization, and lab economics are highly specialized areas where surface-level analysis can be dangerously misleading.

That is why the timing matters so much.

The recent “blunder” was not that demand disappeared. Q1 volume grew strongly, but revenue missed because blended reimbursement came in lower than expected and noncore lines underperformed. The issue appears tied to execution and mix, not a collapse in demand for the core business.

That distinction is everything.

If the story were permanently broken, insiders who have lived inside this company for years would not be adding aggressively right after the market punished the stock.

tweets.text:
Why GeneDx $WGS Became 30% of My Portfolio

I’m going to touch on four aspects of the company in my upcoming posts.

First, insider buying - something you rarely see at this scale, and why here they matter so much.  
-coming later today.

Second, the moat - which is especially rare to find in a fast-growing industry. 
-coming over the weekend 

Third, valuation - the company currently trades at roughly half the industry multiple.
-coming next week

Fourth, the opportunity itself - and why such a disconnect even exists in the first place.
-coming next week

And I hope that by the end, you’ll understand why I allocated 30% of my portfolio to this company. Opportunities like this are incredibly rare: the upside could be measured in multiples of the current price, while the downside appears limited due to the value of its proprietary assets.

tweets.text:
On this day in 1927, Charles Lindbergh landed the Spirit of St. Louis at Le Bourget Field in Paris after 33.5 hours crossing the Atlantic solo. Within weeks, aviation stocks surged over 200%. Companies with "air" or "aircraft" in their name attracted capital regardless of whether they had a functioning airplane. U.S. airline passengers grew from about 6,000 in 1926 to over 173,000 by 1929, a 3,000% increase.

The Lindbergh boom fed directly into the broader market mania of 1928 and 1929. Wright Aeronautical went from $25 to $245 in two years. Curtiss Aeroplane tripled. Investors weren't pricing earnings. They were pricing the future of human transportation, and they weren't wrong about the thesis. They were wrong about the timeline and the valuations.

When the crash hit in October 1929, aviation stocks fell 80% to 90% from their peaks. The industry itself kept growing. Passenger volume kept climbing through the Depression. But the stocks took over a decade to recover. Being right about the technology and wrong about the price is one of the most expensive mistakes in markets.

tweets.text:
Last week CFRA raised its 12-month target for the S&P 500 to 7,730 and year-end 2026 level of 7,575  “reinforced by resilient consumer spending, a stable labor market, an infrastructure  renaissance, and AI-related investment that  is beginning to translate into tangible  revenue growth and productivity gains.”

Sam Stovall, CFRA Chief Investment Strategist also noted that April was the second largest gain for the index since 1945. "[F]ollowing the top  25 gains in April since WWII, the S&P 500 posted a further advance in May, rising 22  times (88%)."

However Stovall also noted that a breather might be due:

As of May 6, the “500’s” Relative Strength Index (RSI) ... closed at 75 and was accompanied by overbought readings for the Nasdaq 100, Communication Services and Information Technology sectors, and 9% of the 155 sub-industries in the S&P 1500.  CFRA thinks a digestion of these recent gains would offer this bull the opportunity to “buy  the dip” and resume its run.

tweets.text:
American Association of Individual Investors (AAII) sees bulls fall to two-month low and under bears who jump to highest since April 2nd:

AAII bulls (those who see higher stock prices in 6 mths, blue line) fell back to 31.7% from 39.3% the prior week, the least since March 19th, and below the long-term historic average of 37.5% for the first time in 12 weeks.

Bulls also fell below the level of the bears (who see lower stock prices in 6 mths, red line) for the first time in three weeks (but the 10th time in 13) as the bears increased to 43.6%, the highest since April 2nd, from 36.6% the prior week. Bears remain above the long-term average of 31.0% for a 14th straight week (and they’ve only been below it 9 weeks since Dec 12, 2024).

The Neutral camp (yellow line) edged up to 24.7% from 24.1% the prior week.  It remains under the long-run average of 31.5% for the 94th week in 96 (since July ‘24).

From the Week Ahead (https://t.co/2PMl3wiejj):

You probably won’t be surprised to know that the term “Middle East” showed up in the conference call transcripts of the S&P 500 companies that conducted earnings conference calls from March 15 through May 15 by far the most in 10 years at 211 or 47% of all the calls according to Factset. The next highest was 85 in Q2 2023.

The Industrials (50) sector had the highest number of earnings calls where the term “Middle East” was cited, while the Energy (90%) sector had the highest percentage of earning calls where the term “Middle East” was cited.

 “However, it is interesting to note that only a small number of the companies that cited 'Middle East' on Q1 earnings calls also lowered EPS guidance for 2026… 60 provided annual EPS guidance that was higher than the previous (annual) guidance issued by the company, 43 maintained previous (annual) EPS guidance, 12 provided annual EPS guidance that was lower than the previous (annual) guidance issued by the company.”

tweets.text:
The Q2 GDP tracker from the Atlanta Fed was updated today and is now up to +4.26%, the highest since it initiated tracking April 30th (remembering that the Q1 number started out very strong but ended up near 1%**) although the changes this month continue to be driven the driven by an increase in inventories which boosted the metric by +0.16% in this update and +0.22% in the last.

The reading remains a world away from the blue chip consensus* of +1.8%.

Here’s the breakdown of the components as of May 21st and changes from my last update May 14th:

Consumption = +1.95% (+0.08%)

Nonresidential fixed investment (biz spending) = +0.85% (-0.06%)

Inventories = +1.06% (+0.16%)

Gov’t = +0.28% (+0.01%)

Net exports = +0.07% (-0.02%)

Residential investment = +0.06% (-0.03%)

*The top (bottom) 10 average forecast is an average of the highest (lowest) 10 forecasts in the Blue Chip survey.

**(as a reminder their Q1 estimate came in -0.75% too low vs the first estimate after their Q4 estimate came in +1.5% too high (both due mostly to not adjusting federal spending appropriately for the shutdown), while Q3 was eight tenths low, but was along with Goldman’s the closest of the trackers for that quarter. Q2 came in just a tenth low (and right in line in Q3 & Q4 ‘24, and a tenth off for Q2 ‘24), but was -1.2% too low for Q1 ‘25)

tweets.text:
BoA: It would have taken a weak April jobs report to keep the balance of risks within a range where Fed Chair Warsh could push cuts through. That isn’t what we got. Private payrolls have picked up and the u-rate is range bound, suggesting downside risks to labor have waned.

In addition, we see rising risks that the core PCE will settle in the 2.5-3% range even after tariff effects roll off. This would also preclude additional rate cuts. 

In our view, the u-rate would have to drop to/below 4%  for hikes to be on the table. We are still some ways away from this outcome.

We remain comfortable with our view that the Fed will be on hold until 2H 2027.

In addition, we see rising risks that the core PCE will settle in the 2.5-3% range even after tariff effects roll off. This would also preclude additional rate cuts.

Myriad $MYRUF / $M is moving into the next phase at Copper Mountain 🔥☢️. 

It's been a long wait, but now the most exciting #uranium drill program (in my opinion) in the U.S. this year is about to start. Phase II drilling starts in June with potential expansion up to 10,000m.

The setup is really interesting:

• Historic 26.6M lbs resource base
• New targets identified by the recent geophysical work and the U.S. Department of Energy "Bendix Report" which estimated an uranium exploration target of up to 655 Mlbs at Copper Mountain will also be tested
• Positive disequilibrium from Phase I suggests grades may be higher than historically reported
• Multiple untested expansion targets across a district-scale system

This is no longer just a historical story — they are systematically validating and expanding it with modern exploration tools. The strategic alliance with Subatomic makes this play even more interesting. Those are really smart guys, and I am quite sure they too have figured out the importance of this project in the not too distant future when the uranium price exceeds $150/lb and the utilities are scrambling for pounds (my expectation).

Disclosure: Paid partnership with Myriad Uranium. This is not financial advice. Please do your own DD.

Those are really smart guys, and I am quite sure they too have figured out the importance of this project in the not too distant future when the uranium price exceeds $150/lb and the utilities are scrambling for pounds (my expectation).

Disclosure: Paid partnership with Myriad Uranium. This is not financial advice. Please do your own DD.

tweets.text:
$IBM IBM was in focus on Thursday after RBC Capital Markets called its deal with the U.S. govt to launch a standalone quantum chip manufacturing company a “big win.” Big Blue doesn't lack for capital, so no clue why the company needed the U.S. govt to invest $1B. 

♦️“We had an opportunity to talk with management who sees this as a big win for IBM and the quantum supply chain noting there is not currently a manufacturer of 300-millimeter wafers of this scale in the U.S.,” RBC Capital analyst Matthew Swanson wrote in a note to clients. “As one of the largest consumers of these wafers, IBM is both securing its own supply chain while also creating a commercial opportunity to be the 'picks and shovels' of the quantum industry. While there is currently no timeline they are disclosing for the completion of Anderon, the investment in domestic 300-millimeter wafer supply does increase the likelihood of IBM being one of the earliest beneficiaries of quantum in our view.”
♦️Swanson has an Outperform rating and $300 price target on IBM.

tweets.text:
$LLY | RBC Capital: 'a clean win for LLY'.

 "This morning, LLY announced top-line results from the P3 TRIUMPH-1 study of retatrutide (LLY's triple G agonist) in overweight/obese adults. The results are in line with what we laid out in our ADA Preview , with the 12mg dose delivering 28.3% mean weight loss (26% pbo-adjusted) at 80 weeks. Further, levels of weight loss did not plateau, with weight loss deepening to 30.3% through the 104-week extension period, a meaningful durability differentiator, in our view. The low-dose 4 mg arm also impressed, achieving 19.0% weight loss with just a single escalation step, outperforming/in line with currently marketed GLP-1 therapies. On tolerability, the 12mg discontinuation rate of only 11.3% came in below expectations, and critically, no new safety signals emerged, particularly on the CV side, which had been a primary investor overhang given retatrutide's glucagon agonism component. The clean safety profile, combined with best-in-class efficacy across all doses, makes this a clean win for LLY. Full results will be presented at ADA on June 6th and we will be hosting a KOL breakfast event on Monday, June 8 at 8am to discuss the full data set, among others."

The results are in line with what we laid out in our ADA Preview , with the 12mg dose delivering 28.3% mean weight loss (26% pbo-adjusted) at 80 weeks. Further, levels of weight loss did not plateau, with weight loss deepening to 30.3% through the 104-week extension period, a meaningful durability differentiator, in our view. The low-dose 4 mg arm also impressed, achieving 19.0% weight loss with just a single escalation step, outperforming/in line with currently marketed GLP-1 therapies. On tolerability, the 12mg discontinuation rate of only 11.3% came in below expectations, and critically, no new safety signals emerged, particularly on the CV side, which had been a primary investor overhang given retatrutide's glucagon agonism component. The clean safety profile, combined with best-in-class efficacy across all doses, makes this a clean win for LLY. Full results will be presented at ADA on June 6th and we will be hosting a KOL breakfast event on Monday, June 8 at 8am to discuss the full data set, among others."

$ALGM - EVERCORE ISI LIFTS PT TO $53 AND ALLEGRO IS BACK ON THE L3-AUTO-CONTENT BID

Allegro MicroSystems +6.4% on the day to $44.33. Evercore ISI maintains Outperform, raises PT to $53 from $49. FY26 revenue closed at $890.1M from $725M prior year; FY27 Q1 guide $245-255M revenue with EPS $0.19-$0.23.

The Hesai L3 print from this week named ALGM-class auto SoC suppliers as the downstream beneficiaries: L3 architectures need sensor-fusion compute, current-sensing ICs, magnetic position sensors — Allegro's exact stack. Per-vehicle lidar content stepping from $200 to $500-1,000 means the chips around the lidar also step up.

Mechanism: L3 mandates more silicon per car, full stop. Allegro sits on the analog/sensing side of that ramp, which has historically lagged the headline auto-OEM tape by a quarter or two.

$META Why do we like it here? - Evercore ISI

First, fighting a narrative could take time to work - we may need to remain a bit patient. We are buyers because: a) Valuation is highly attractive - at 16x '27 EPS (vs. Google at 27x '27 EPS), this is one of the widest valuation gaps we've seen and Meta is arguably a higher quality asset than most of S&P 500, while it trades at a material discount. PT $800
